questionText: Two drones fly so that, at time $t$ seconds ($t \ge 0$), their positions in metres are: drone A at $x = 3t$, $y = t^{2}$; drone B at $x = 2t + 2$, $y = 5t - 6$. (a) Determine whether the drones collide (same place at the same time); if so, find when and where. (b) Find the speed of each drone at that instant.

steps.explanation: A collision needs both drones at the same place at the same time, so first we require their $x$-coordinates to be equal.

steps.description: Solve for t from the x-equation
steps.explanation: The horizontal condition gives a single candidate time, $t = 2$, at which the drones share the same $x$-coordinate.
For a genuine collision the $y$-coordinates must also agree at the same time, so we set the two heights equal.
Bringing all terms to one side produces a quadratic whose roots are the times when the heights match.
The heights coincide at $t = 2$ and $t = 3$; we must see which of these also satisfies the horizontal condition.

steps.description: Select the common time and reject the other
steps.workingLatex: t = 2 \text{ satisfies both}; \quad t = 3:\; x_A = 9,\; x_B = 8 \Rightarrow \text{reject}
steps.explanation: Only $t = 2$ satisfies both the $x$- and $y$-conditions. At $t = 3$ the drones are at the same height but different horizontal positions ($9$ versus $8$), so that is not a collision and is rejected.

steps.description: Find drone A's position at t = 2
steps.explanation: Substituting $t=2$ into drone A's equations gives its location at that instant.

steps.description: Find drone B's position at t = 2
steps.explanation: Doing the same for drone B gives the identical point $(6,4)$, confirming a genuine collision there.
For the speeds we differentiate each drone's position; drone A has a constant horizontal velocity and a vertical velocity that grows with time.

steps.description: Speed of drone A at t = 2
steps.explanation: Evaluating $\dot{y}_A = 4$ at $t=2$ and combining with $\dot{x}_A = 3$ by Pythagoras gives drone A's speed of $5$ metres per second.
Drone B moves along straight-line equations, so both its velocity components are constant.
Combining drone B's constant components gives its speed $\sqrt{29}$ metres per second, unchanging throughout.
The drones do collide, at $(6,4)$ when $t=2$; their differing speeds ($5$ and $\sqrt{29}\approx 5.39$) show they are not travelling together, they simply meet at that instant.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: The drones collide at the point $(6,\,4)$ when $t = 2$ s (the time $t=3$ is rejected because the heights match there but the horizontal positions do not). At that instant drone A has speed $5\text{ m s}^{-1}$ and drone B has speed $\sqrt{29} \approx 5.39\text{ m s}^{-1}$.
